title: Leek and potato soup
subtitle: Silky British soup from two of the most reliable vegetables.
excerpt: Leeks sweated gently in butter with potato, simmered in stock until soft and blended to a smooth, pale green soup. Foundational British cooking at its quietest. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Leek and potato soup. Leeks have been cultivated in Britain and France since antiquity and appear throughout both cooking traditions. The simple combination of leek, potato and stock is documented in British cookbooks throughout the 20th century. The French variant vichyssoise (chilled, with cream) was created at the Ritz-Carlton New York in 1917 by chef Louis Diat. The hot British version is simpler and older.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the cream. Add cream when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. A good batch-cook soup.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead; refrigerate. Reheat gently.

content.text: Leek and potato soup is made well or made badly based on one step: how long you cook the leeks before the stock goes in. Five minutes is not enough. Twelve minutes over low heat, until the leeks are completely soft and sweet, makes the difference between a flat soup and a silky one. Do not rush the sweating stage.

items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, sliced and washed

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over low heat. Add {{leek}} of sliced leeks and {{onion}} of onion. Cook, stirring occasionally, for 12 to 15 minutes until completely soft but not coloured. Add {{potato}} of potato and {{stock-vegetable}} of stock. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes until the potato is completely tender.
Blend until completely smooth. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream if using. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately, or cool and refrigerate.

items.cause: Leeks not sweated long enough, or stock too weak.
items.fix: Sweat leeks until they are completely soft and beginning to show sweetness. Use a good-quality stock.

items.symptom: Soup has gritty texture after blending
items.cause: Grit from the leeks not washed out.
items.fix: Slice the leeks, separate the rings and wash thoroughly in a colander. Grit between the layers is the most common cause of a grainy soup.

items.symptom: Soup discoloured to a dull grey
items.cause: Green tops included, or blended too long at high speed.
items.fix: Use only the white and pale green parts of the leek. Blend briefly — over-processing discolours the soup.

content.text: Leek and potato soup is British kitchen cooking at its most dependable: a handful of ingredients, good technique and nothing exotic. It is a lunchtime staple from October to March, warming and sustaining without being heavy. The same base can be made into vichyssoise by chilling it and adding more cream, or into cock-a-leekie by adding chicken and prunes.
